flashcards.front: What is an array in C++?
flashcards.back: An array is a collection of elements of the same data type stored in contiguous memory locations, accessed using indices.

flashcards.front: What is the base index of C++ arrays?
flashcards.back: C++ arrays are zero-indexed, meaning the first element is at index 0, second at index 1, and so on.

flashcards.front: How do you declare a 1D array in C++?
flashcards.back: Syntax: data_type array_name[array_size]; Example: int marks[5]; declares an array of 5 integers.

flashcards.front: What is the difference between if and if-else statements?
flashcards.back: if executes code only when condition is true; if-else executes one block when true and another block when false.

flashcards.front: What is the purpose of the switch statement?
flashcards.back: switch provides multi-way branching based on the value of an expression, testing against multiple constant values.

flashcards.front: What is the key difference between while and do-while loops?
flashcards.back: while checks condition before loop body (may not execute); do-while checks after (executes at least once).

flashcards.front: What does the break statement do in loops?
flashcards.back: break terminates the loop immediately and transfers control to the statement after the loop.

flashcards.front: What does the continue statement do in loops?
flashcards.back: continue skips the current iteration and moves to the next iteration of the loop.
A multi-dimensional array has more than one dimension, commonly 2D arrays represented as rows and columns (matrix).
Array initialization assigns initial values at declaration: int arr[3] = {1, 2, 3}; or int arr[] = {1, 2, 3};
A nested loop is a loop inside another loop, commonly used for traversing multi-dimensional structures.
A loop that never terminates because its condition always remains true; must be broken using break or external intervention.

flashcards.front: What is the syntax of a for loop in C++?
flashcards.back: for(initialization; condition; increment/decrement) { // statements } Example: for(int i=0; i<5; i++)

flashcards.front: What is array bounds checking in C++?
flashcards.back: C++ does not perform automatic bounds checking; accessing invalid indices causes undefined behavior (memory corruption).

flashcards.front: When is switch preferred over if-else?
flashcards.back: switch is preferred when comparing a single variable against multiple constant values for cleaner, more readable code.
